I'm sorry to hear that. Could you tell me what dilution ratio and application method were used?
The dilution ratio was according to your instructions, one unit for a bucket of water.
Thank you. Approximately how much water was in the bucket?
A bucket for the sprayer; 10 liters of water.
Thank you for the information. To help investigate further, please provide the product name.
There's nothing more to say. The weeds didn’t die, but the rapeseed did. What is the reason for this? I don’t want to pursue the matter further, just refund my purchase.

Based on the information you provided, you used 1 sachet (designed for 15 kg of water) in only 10 liters of water. This created a solution approximately 50% more concentrated than the recommended rate. For (50% Benazolin), over-concentration of this herbicide can cause phytotoxicity to the rapeseed crop itself, which is the likely reason the crop was damaged while weeds survived. For future use, always dilute 1 sachet in the full 15 kg of water. Regarding your current order, please provide your order number and we will process your refund request.

Yes, you can use it for soil cultivation.
Can it be used for succulents? How do I use soil cultivation?

Yes, it works for succulents too. Here's how to use it: Our store's water-soluble fertilizers and microbial agents are typically used by mixing 1 gram with 0.5 liters of water. The water-soluble fertilizer is used for root application, while the microbial agent can be applied by root drenching or foliar spraying, depending on the location of the disease in your crops. For pesticides, mix 5 grams with 0.5 liters of water for foliar spraying. Regular maintenance can be done with clean water. The cap of a 30-gram container holds approximately 4-7 grams. The cap of a 500-gram container holds approximately 30 grams (mix with 15 liters of water). The cap of a 200-gram container holds approximately 15 grams. If you need to use other pesticides or plant medicines, it is advisable to use them separately. It is best to apply in the morning before 10 AM.

The tomato leaves have started curling. Will this pesticide be effective?

Tomato leaf curling can be physiological or pathological. It's important to first identify the type and then address it appropriately for more effective results: 1. Quick Identification (Key Distinction) - Physiological Leaf Curling (most common): Entire plant or multiple leaves curling upwards or downwards with no spots or insect droppings, often due to environmental or management issues. - Pathological Leaf Curling: Localized leaf curling with yellowing, spots, deformities, insect holes, or droppings, often caused by viruses or pests. 2. Physiological Leaf Curling (Adjust Environment & Management) - High Temperature/Strong Light or Drought (Leaves curling upwards, becoming brittle): - Shading: Use shade nets (30%-50%) at noon to avoid direct sunlight. - Watering: Water thoroughly in the morning and evening to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged. Spray leaves with water to cool them down (avoid noon sprays). - Soil Loosening: Loosen topsoil slightly to improve soil aeration. - Overwatering/Waterlogging (Leaves curling downwards, yellowing, root rot): - Water Control: Stop watering temporarily, loosen the soil, and ensure ventilation to speed up soil drying. - Root Treatment: If root rot is severe, remove the plant from the pot, trim rotten roots, disinfect with carbendazim, and replant in loose soil. - Improper Fertilization (Fertilizer Damage/Nutrient Deficiency): - Fertilizer Damage (Leaf edges scorched, curling): Drench the potting mix with water to dilute the fertilizer; pause fertilization for 1-2 weeks. - Nutrient Deficiency (Potassium/Boron Deficiency, Leaf puckering, and deformities): Supplement with potassium dihydrogen phosphate (foliar spray at a 1:1000 ratio) and boron fertilizer every 7-10 days. - Improper Pruning (Top young leaves curling): Avoid over-pruning at once; do it gradually. Apply carbendazim to pruning wounds to prevent infections. 3. Pathological Leaf Curling (Targeted Medication) - Viral Diseases (Leaf puckering, yellow-green mottling, plant dwarfing): - No specific cure available. First prevent vector-borne transmission: use imidacloprid or acetamiprid to control aphids and whiteflies. - Deal with infected plants: Severely infected plants should be removed and destroyed to prevent spread. Mildly infected plants can be sprayed with amino-oligosaccharin or mushroom polysaccharide to enhance resistance. - Pest Infestation (Aphids/Whiteflies/Yellow Mites, Insects or honeydew on the underside of leaves): - Aphids/Whiteflies: Use imidacloprid, thiamethoxam; apply every 7 days for 2-3 rounds. - Yellow mites (Leaves shiny, stiff, curling on the underside): Use avermectin or abamectin, focusing on the underside of the leaves. - Fungal/Bacterial Diseases (With Spots, e.g., Late Blight, Bacterial Wilt): - Late Blight (Leaves with brown spots, moist and white mold): Use cymoxanil hydrochloride or fluazinam. - Bacterial Wilt (Plants suddenly wilt, stem vascular bundles turn brown): Use thiazole zinc or kasugamycin, applied to roots and leaves. 4. General Prevention (Reduce Recurrence) - Maintain good ventilation and light, avoid prolonged wetting of leaves. - Regularly apply protective agents (e.g., mancozeb, chlorothalonil) every 7-10 days. - Timely removal of diseased leaves and weeds to reduce sources of pest and pathogen infection.

Is there a way to save them?

Core Symptoms: Bacterial Disease: The withering of branches and black, dry leaves are typical signs of bacterial pith necrosis. The bacteria enter through wounds, causing internal stem rot, blocking nutrient transport, and leading to branch wilt. Apply Fungicide: Spray a solution of thiazole zinc (800 times dilution) and polyoxin (600 times dilution), focusing on the stem and new shoots every 7 days for 2 consecutive sprays. Supplement and Revitalize: Spray a foliar application of fluid boron and sugar alcohol calcium (1000 times dilution) every 10 days for 2 consecutive sprays to enhance branch resistance. Also, control watering to avoid waterlogging in the potting soil.

Is this usable?

If the edges of strawberry leaves are turning black and drying, the core issue is the obstruction in root absorption along with environmental or management issues. Prioritize checking these four points: 1. The most common: Root problems (suffocated roots/rotten roots/weak roots). Cause: Waterlogged, compact soil with poor aeration, or overly frequent watering leading to root oxygen shortage and decay, hindering water absorption, causing the leaf tips/edges to blacken and dry. Identification: Persistently wet soil with an odor. Look at the roots upon uprooting → they appear black/soft and rotten. Solution: Control water + Loosen soil: Pause watering, gently loosen the topsoil, and ventilate to speed up drying; In severe cases, repot: Cut rotten roots, disinfect with carbendazim, and replant in loose soil (mix of garden soil, leaf mold, and perlite in a 3:4:3 ratio). 2. Fertilizer damage (Improper fertilization) Cause: Strong or raw fertilizers applied too frequently, or high concentration of foliar fertilizer, burning roots/leaves, and causing blackened, charred leaf edges. Identification: Occurs 1-3 days after fertilization, with wrinkled leaves and crispy edges. Solution: Immediately flush the soil thoroughly with water to dilute the fertilizer; Stop fertilization for 1-2 months, lightly fertilize after recovery. 3. Dry/Hot Environment/Cold Wind/Intense Sunlight Cause: Dry air, high temperature (>30°C), direct cold wind (near windows/air conditioning), or sudden intense sunlight causes leaf dehydration and blackened edges. Identification: Multiple plants show the same symptoms, without odor, with normal soil moisture. Solution: Place in an area with diffused light and good ventilation, away from heat/cold sources; Spray water in the surrounding area for humidity without spraying the leaf centers during daytime. 4. Disease (Anthracnose/Leaf spot/Blight) Cause: High humidity, poor ventilation, and fungal/bacterial infections, causing blackened leaf edges extending inward, with spots and decay. Identification: Brown disease spots on leaves, wet with fungal layers, spreading gradually. Solution: Trim affected leaves and dispose of them; Spray with Mancozeb or Chlorothalonil every 7-10 days, in 2-3 consecutive applications. Quick Assessment Steps Feel the soil: If wet→control water and loosen soil; if dry→water thoroughly; Evaluate: Any recent fertilization/repotting or sun exposure→apply corresponding treatment; Examine leaves: Spots/fungal layers→disease; none→environment/root issues. Subsequent Care Watering: Water thoroughly when dry, avoiding waterlogging, and ensure no water accumulates at the leaf center; Fertilization: Light and frequent, using specialized strawberry fertilizer, halving the concentration; Environment: Maintain good ventilation, temperature between 15-25°C, and avoid sudden cold or heat.

What weeds can be controlled in rapeseed fields?

Applicable range and main usage Applicable rapeseed types: Brassica napus (European rapeseed): Strong herbicide resistance, safe under normal usage; Brassica rapa and Brassica juncea (Asian types): Relatively sensitive, strictly control dosage (suggest to test with half a dose first) to avoid phytotoxicity. Best application timing: Direct-seeded rapeseed: At 3-6 leaf stage; grass weeds at 2-5 leaf stage; Transplanted rapeseed: 7-10 days after recovery from transplanting (new leaf emergence), same weed leaf stage as above; Forbidden periods: Do not apply before the 2-leaf stage of rapeseed or during the bolting stage. Effectiveness decreases, and plants are more prone to damage under low temperatures (<8°C). Dosage and method: Brassica napus: Use 20-30 ml of 24% clethodim emulsifiable concentrate per 666.67 sqm , mixed with 30 kg of water for foliar spray; Brassica rapa / Brassica juncea: Use 15-20 ml per 666.67 sqm . Can clethodim be used for grass weeds in rapeseed seedlings?

24% clethodim can be used in rapeseed fields, but it is only effective against grass weeds (such as barnyard grass, crabgrass, and Italian ryegrass), and not against broadleaf weeds. It's important to consider the safety of the rapeseed variety: 1. Scope of Application and Core Usage Applicable Rapeseed Types: Brassica napus: Highly resistant, safe for normal use; Brassica rapa and Brassica juncea varieties: Relatively sensitive, dosage must be strictly controlled (suggested to halve the dosage for trial), to avoid phytotoxicity. Optimal Application Timing: Direct-seeded rapeseed: 3-6 leaf stage for rapeseed, 2-5 leaf stage for grass weeds; Transplanted rapeseed: 7-10 days after transplanting (when new leaves emerge), with the same leaf stage for weeds; Prohibited periods: Do not apply before the 2-leaf stage or during the bolting stage of rapeseed, as efficacy is poor and seedling injury is more likely at temperatures below 8°C. Usage and Method: Brassica napus: Use 20-30 ml of 24% clethodim emulsifiable concentrate per 666.67 sqm , mixed with 30 kg of water for foliar spraying; Brassica rapa/Brassica juncea: Use 15-20 ml per 666.67 sqm , initially try on a small area to ensure no damage occurs before broadening use. 2. Precautions If the field contains mixed grass and broadleaf weeds, clethodim can be tank-mixed with quizalofop (only for Brassica napus) to achieve dual control; Ensure good ventilation after application to prevent drift onto other sensitive crops; Use a maximum of 1 time per season, with a safety interval of 30 days (cease application 30 days before rapeseed harvest).

What medicine should be used for leaf curl?

Tomato leaf curl can be physiological or pathological. Determine the type first for more effective treatment: 1. Quick assessment (core distinction) - Physiological leaf curl (most common): Occurs across the entire plant or multiple plants with upward or downward curling, without blight marks or insect droppings, often due to environmental or management issues. - Pathological leaf curl: Localized leaf curling, accompanied by yellowing, spots, deformation, insect holes, or droppings, usually caused by viral diseases or pests. 2. Physiological Leaf Curl (adjust environment and management) - High temperature, strong light/drought (leaves curl upwards, become brittle): - Shade: Use shade nets (30%-50%) at noon to prevent sunburn. - Watering: Thorough irrigation in the morning and evening to keep the soil moist but not waterlogged; spray foliage with water to cool, avoiding midday; loosen the topsoil to enhance soil aeration. - Excessive watering/waterlogging (leaves curl downwards, turn yellow, roots rot): - Water control: Halt watering, loosen soil for ventilation to accelerate drying. - Root rescue: If root rot is severe, unpot and trim rotten roots, disinfect with carbendazim, and replant in loose soil. - Improper fertilization (fertilizer burn/lack of nutrients): - Fertilizer burn (leaf edges scorch, curl): Flush potting soil with large amounts of water to dilute fertilizer; suspend fertilization for 1-2 weeks. - Nutrient deficiency (potassium/boron shortage, leaves wrinkle, deform): Supplement with monopotassium phosphate (foliar spray at 1:1000) + boron fertilizer every 7-10 days. - Improper pruning (top young leaves curl): - Avoid pruning too much at once, do it gradually; apply carbendazim to wounds after pruning to prevent infections. 3. Pathological Leaf Curl (targeted treatment) - Viral diseases (leaves wrinkle, mosaic yellow-green, plant stunts): - No specific treatment; first prevent the vector: Use imidacloprid/dinotefuran to spray for aphids and whiteflies. - Treat affected plants: Severely affected plants should be removed and destroyed to prevent spread; lightly affected plants should be sprayed with amino-oligosaccharides/mushroom polysaccharides to enhance resistance. - Pest infestation (aphids/whiteflies/red spider mites, insects/honeydew present on underside of leaves): - Aphids/whiteflies: Use imidacloprid or thiamethoxam, every 7 days, continue for 2-3 applications. - Red spider mites (underside of leaves shiny, stiff, curled): Use abamectin or bifenazate, focus on spraying underside of leaves. - Fungal/Bacterial diseases (with spots, e.g., late blight, bacterial wilt): - Late blight (brown spots on leaves, wet with white mold): Use dimethomorph hydrochloride, and fludioxonil. - Bacterial wilt (plant wilts suddenly, vascular bundles in stem brown): Use thiazole zinc and kasugamycin, apply to soil and spray foliage. 4. General Prevention (reduce recurrence) - Maintain good ventilation and light penetration, avoid leaves being wet for extended periods. - Regularly spray protective agents (mancozeb, chlorothalonil), every 7-10 days. - Promptly remove diseased leaves and weeds to reduce pest and pathogen sources.
